original_hypothesis.title: setMaxSellPercent Has No Upper Bound Allowing Token Issuer to Bypass Sell Restrictions
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: VestingManager.setMaxSellPercent accepts any uint256 value without upper bound validation. The tokenIssuer can set maxSellPercent to values far exceeding BASE (10000), effectively allowing all original allocation holders to sell 100% of their vesting, bypassing the intended supply restriction mechanism.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The function has no require statement limiting maxSellPercent to <= BASE. The sell limit formula `((currentAlloc + sold - bought) * maxSellPercent) / BASE` with maxSellPercent > BASE would yield a limit exceeding the original allocation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: function setMaxSellPercent(address vesting, uint256 maxSellPercent) external — no require(maxSellPercent <= BASE)

preconditions: Caller is the tokenIssuer of the specific vesting plan

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: No upper bound check on maxSellPercent
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: maxSellPercent = type(uint256).max allows 100%+ of allocation to be sold

analysis_questions: Can selling more than 100% cause underflows in allocation tracking?
Does this affect the VestingManager's available balance calculations?
Can this be exploited by the tokenIssuer to manipulate token markets?
validation_requirements: Set maxSellPercent to 20000, verify user can sell > 100% of original allocation
Verify available() balance constraints still prevent actual over-transfer

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Token issuer sets maxSellPercent to 50000 (500%). Users can sell up to 500% of their original allocation if they have the tokens. While available() limits actual transfers, the sell limit tracking becomes meaningless, undermining the protocol's supply control mechanism.
prerequisites: Token issuer intentionally or accidentally sets high maxSellPercent

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Secondary market supply controls bypassed; potential token price impact
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All token holders in the affected vesting plan
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Token issuer can reset to correct value

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: Missing boundary test for maxSellPercent values

validation_result.mechanism: missing upper bound validation on percentage parameter
validation_result.impact_summary: setMaxSellPercent accepts any uint256 without upper bound check, allowing tokenIssuer to bypass sell restrictions (moderate values > BASE) or cause DoS via overflow (extreme values).
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The function setMaxSellPercent in SecondSwap_VestingManager.sol at lines 109-112 accepts any uint256 value without validating that it is <= BASE (10000). This is inconsistent with every other percentage/fee parameter in the system, all of which have upper bound checks. When maxSellPercent > BASE, the sell limit formula in listVesting yields a value exceeding the original allocation, making the percentage restriction meaningless. When maxSellPercent is extremely large (e.g., type(uint256).max), the multiplication in listVesting overflows in Solidity 0.8.x, causing a revert and DoS for listing operations.

The available() check does prevent selling more tokens than actually held, but the maxSellPercent mechanism exists specifically to impose a stricter limit than the available balance. Bypassing it defeats the protocol's supply control mechanism for the secondary market.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Lines 109-112 of SecondSwap_VestingManager.sol confirm no upper bound validation exists. The function only checks that the caller is the tokenIssuer of the vesting plan. Comparing with setBuyerFee (line 139: require(_fee >= -1 && _fee <= 5000)), setSellerFee (line 149: require(_fee >= -1 && _fee <= 5000)), and MarketplaceSetting.setBuyerFee (require(_amount <= 5000)), every other percentage parameter has bounded validation. The listVesting function at lines 68-85 shows how maxSellPercent is used in a formula where values > BASE produce sell limits exceeding original allocations.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: No require(maxSellPercent <= BASE) or similar upper bound check. Any uint256 value is accepted. Values > 10000 bypass sell restrictions; extremely large values cause overflow DoS in listVesting.

When maxSellPercent > BASE (10000), this formula produces sellLimit values exceeding the original allocation. E.g., maxSellPercent=20000 yields sellLimit = bought + 2*(originalAlloc), allowing 200% sell of original. With maxSellPercent=type(uint256).max, the multiplication overflows and reverts.

calling_sequence: 1. tokenIssuer calls setMaxSellPercent(vestingAddress, 20000) — no upper bound check, value accepted
3. User calls Marketplace.listVesting() which calls VestingManager.listVesting()
4. In listVesting, sellLimit calculation: sellLimit += ((currentAlloc + sold - bought) * 20000) / 10000 = 2 * originalAlloc
5. User can now sell up to 200% of their original allocation (limited only by available() balance)
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: setMaxSellPercent stores arbitrary value → listVesting uses it in multiplication with BASE as denominator → values > BASE produce sell limits exceeding original allocation percentage intent → the sell percentage restriction is effectively bypassed

config_validation.deployment_evidence: Deployment scripts show setSellable defaults maxSellPercent to 2000 (20%) on first enable, but setMaxSellPercent has no deployment-time configuration. The defect is in the contract code itself.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Token issuer (accidentally or maliciously) calls setMaxSellPercent(vestingAddr, 50000). This sets maxSellPercent to 500%. For a user with 1000 tokens original allocation and 0 bought, sellLimit becomes 0 + (1000 * 50000) / 10000 = 5000. Since available() limits actual transfer to their real balance (~1000), they can sell 100% of their original allocation instead of the intended restricted percentage. The protocol's supply control mechanism is completely bypassed. Alternatively, setting maxSellPercent to type(uint256).max causes overflow in the multiplication, reverting all listing attempts — a DoS attack on the vesting plan.
validation_result.impact_assessment: Medium severity. The missing validation allows the tokenIssuer to bypass the sell percentage restriction mechanism, undermining the protocol's secondary market supply controls. While this requires the tokenIssuer's action (semi-trusted role), it represents a code defect inconsistent with all other percentage parameters in the system. Additionally, extremely large values can cause DoS via overflow. The available() check prevents actual over-transfer of tokens but does not preserve the intended percentage-based restriction.
recommended_mitigations: Add require(maxSellPercent <= BASE, "SS_VestingManager: maxSellPercent cannot exceed 100%") in setMaxSellPercent
Consider adding require(maxSellPercent > 0) to also prevent setting to zero (which would prevent all selling)
Consider adding overflow-safe multiplication or an explicit check that the multiplication won't overflow in listVesting
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy full system with vesting plan. 2. Create vesting for userA with 1000 tokens. 3. As tokenIssuer, call setMaxSellPercent(vestingAddr, 20000). 4. As userA, list 1000 tokens (100% of allocation). 5. Verify listing succeeds despite maxSellPercent intent being a percentage cap. 6. Repeat with type(uint256).max and verify overflow revert in listVesting.
uncertainties: Whether the tokenIssuer role is expected to be fully trusted or semi-trusted in the threat model (code treats it as semi-trusted based on other trust boundary analysis)
additional_testing_needed: Test setMaxSellPercent with boundary values: 0, BASE-1, BASE, BASE+1, type(uint256).max
Test listVesting behavior with maxSellPercent > BASE to confirm sell limit exceeds original allocation
Test listVesting with maxSellPercent = type(uint256).max to confirm overflow revert
